title: Module 4 quiz: Security at the edge

questions.prompt: What's automatic vs. tunable in Cloudflare DDoS protection?

choices.text: L3/L4 (network volumetric) protection is automatic, always on, unmetered. L7 (HTTP) protection has automatic signatures plus app-specific tuning needed via WAF rules, rate limits, and bot management. Volumetric absorbed for you; targeted attacks you wire up.

questions.explanation: Volumetric DDoS is structurally absorbed. Targeted L7 / app-specific attacks need explicit WAF + rate limits + bot management.

questions.prompt: Deploying a new WAF rule that blocks empty User-Agents. Right approach?

choices.text: Log mode first; monitor Firewall Events for false positives (legitimate UA-less traffic — internal monitoring, mobile apps, etc.); refine; promote to Block only when confident. Ship-and-pray block rules regularly cause customer outages.

questions.explanation: Log → monitor → refine → Block is the canonical WAF rollout. Without monitoring, false positives are invisible until customers complain.

questions.prompt: Your auth endpoint is being credential-stuffed across thousands of residential proxy IPs. Per-IP rate limit isn't catching it. Right fix?

choices.text: Layered defense: bot score < 30 challenges, rate limit by username (in addition to IP), Turnstile on the login form, backend account-lock on N failed attempts. Per-IP only fails when attackers spread across many IPs; per-account and bot-aware layers catch what IP-based can't.

questions.explanation: Credential stuffing across many IPs needs account-aware and bot-aware defenses. Stack: bot score, per-account rate limit, CAPTCHA, backend lock. Each catches what the others miss.

questions.prompt: An attacker DDoS'd your origin directly despite Cloudflare being in front. Most common cause?

choices.text: Origin IP leak (mail server on same IP, CT logs, old DNS history) combined with no origin firewall lockdown. Mitigations: split mail to different IP, audit for leaks (CT logs are public), lock origin firewall to Cloudflare IP ranges. Even with leaks, firewall lockdown makes the IP useless to attackers.

questions.explanation: Origin IP leaks are common. Firewall lockdown to Cloudflare IP ranges is the structural defense — even if IP leaks, attackers can't connect.

questions.prompt: What's the correct policy for Cloudflare API tokens in CI/CD?

choices.text: One account-wide admin token for convenience.
Least-privilege per use case: separate tokens scoped to specific zones with minimum permissions per job. Rotate periodically; audit usage. Account-wide tokens turn a single leak into full compromise.

questions.explanation: Cloudflare API tokens support fine-grained permissions. Each automation gets only what it needs. Limits blast radius of leaks; supports audit and rotation.
